channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Thanks to an Old-Fashioned Family Novel, This 22-Year-Old is Already a Literary Star in Europe
item.description: Just 22 and still a student, Nelio Biedermann has been compared to Thomas Mann thanks to “Lázár,” his sweeping family saga.
item.dc:creator: Thomas Rogers
item.media:credit: Clara Watt for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Most people expect a young person’s first book to be about their own life,” says Nelio Biedermann about the reaction to “Lázár.”

item.title: Book Review: ‘Passport Photo Service,’ by Philip Sharkey
item.description: In 10 minutes or less, this mom-and-pop London institution produced stylish snapshots for some of the world’s biggest stars.
item.dc:creator: Erica Ackerberg
item.category: Passport Photo Service: An Unexpected Archive of Celebrity Portraits (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Lázár,’ by Nelio Biedermann
item.description: “Lázár,” by Nelio Biedermann, is a multigenerational novel that spans the collapse of a monarchy, two world wars and a revolution.
item.dc:creator: Elvia Wilk

item.title: Book Review: ‘A Terrible Intimacy,’ by Melvin Patrick Ely
item.description: A new book by the historian Melvin Patrick Ely draws on court records to highlight the complex relationships between enslavers and the enslaved.
item.dc:creator: Kate Masur
item.category: A Terrible Intimacy: Interracial Life in the Slaveholding South (Book)
item.media:description: Ely convincingly reveals how the antebellum social order was defined both by violent white supremacy and by a surprising variety of interracial relationships.

item.title: The 10 Best Books of 2025
item.description: The staff of The New York Times Book Review choose the year’s top fiction and nonfiction.
item.category: The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny (Book)
There Is No Place for Us: Working and Homeless in America (Book)
Mother Emanuel: Two Centuries of Race, Resistance, and Forgiveness in One Charleston Church (Book)
A Marriage at Sea: A True Story of Love, Obsession, and Shipwreck (Book)
Wild Thing: A Life of Paul Gauguin (Book)

item.title: Lena Dunham Is Still Trying to Figure Out Why People Hated Her So Much
item.description: The writer, actor and lightning rod is not done sharing yet.
item.dc:creator: David Marchese
item.media:credit: Devin Oktar Yalkin for The New York Times

item.title: Book Review: ‘EMPIRE OF SKULLS’ by Paul Stob
item.description: In “Empire of Skulls,” Paul Stob explores how a mania took over America.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Wright
item.category: Empire of Skulls: Phrenology, the Fowler Family and a New Nation's Quest to Unlock the Secrets of the Mind (Book)
item.media:description: The popular theory of phrenology held that there are 37 portions of the brain, each responsible for a certain temperament.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Monuments of Paris,’ by Violaine Huisman
item.description: After devoting her first novel to her wild mother, Violaine Huisman focuses her second on her father, a man who amassed wealth, love affairs and stories.
item.dc:creator: Naomi Huffman

item.title: How Authors and Readers Feel About the ‘Shy Girl’ Cancellation
item.description: Major publishing houses risk unwittingly putting out books generated with A.I. tools. Authors and readers are frustrated, nervous and grasping for solutions.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Oracle’s Daughter,’ by Harrison Hill
item.description: In his excellent “The Oracle’s Daughter,” Harrison Hill looks at the people and the questions beyond the headlines.
item.dc:creator: Carlene Bauer
item.category: The Oracle's Daughter: The Rise and Fall of an American Cult (Book)
item.media:credit: Cibola County Sheriff's Office, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Deborah Green presided over the Aggressive Christianity Missions Training Corps, a militaristic commune complete with uniforms and ranks.

item.title: Book Review: ‘When Tomorrow Burns,’ by Tae Keller
item.description: Tae Keller’s new novel, “When Tomorrow Burns,” offers reassuring answers to the question, “What do you do when your biggest fear comes true?”
item.dc:creator: Craig Morgan Teicher

item.title: Oscar Wilde’s Grandson Reckons With the Legacy of Scandal
item.description: Merlin Holland has spent decades dismantling the myths that grew up around his grandfather. He hopes his new book may finally settle the record.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Winkler
item.category: After Oscar: The Legacy of a Scandal (Book)
item.media:credit: Clara Watt for The New York Times
item.media:description: “So much after his death was invented one way or another,” Merlin Holland says of his grandfather, Oscar Wilde.

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.
item.category: London Falling: A Mysterious Death in a Gilded City and a Family's Search for Truth (Book)
Here Where We Live Is Our Country: The Story of the Jewish Bund (Book)

item.title: If You Love ‘The Pitt,’ You’ll Love These Memoirs by Real E.R. Doctors
item.description: Memoirs from the front lines capture the high-octane pace, roller coaster stakes and unforgettable personalities of emergency medicine.
item.dc:creator: Tina Jordan and Elisabeth Egan

item.title: The Hit Erotica Writers Outwitting Nigeria’s Religious Censors
item.description: Zealous officials burned their predecessors’ romance novels. Now, young Muslim women in northern Nigeria publish their erotic books in installments on WhatsApp.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean, Ismail Auwal and Taiwo Aina
item.media:credit: Taiwo Aina for The New York Times

item.title: Best Alien Books by Octavia E. Butler, Ted Chiang and More
item.description: In these science fiction books, extraterrestrial beings are sympathetic, horrifying and everything in between.
item.dc:creator: James S.A. Corey

item.title: Book Review: ‘London Falling,’ by Patrick Radden Keefe
item.description: A new book by Patrick Radden Keefe retraces the secret life of a 19-year-old Londoner who fell in with a gangster underworld.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.category: London Falling: A Mysterious Death in a Gilded City and a Family's Search for Truth (Book)
item.media:description: The Riverwalk luxury residential complex in London, where Zac Brettler fell from a fifth-floor apartment under mysterious circumstances in 2019.

item.title: Poetry Review: ‘Creature Feature,’ by Dean Young
item.description: Dean Young’s posthumous collection, “Creature Feature,” applies his characteristically giddy sense of unraveling to his own life and ill health.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Gordinier

item.title: Fanciful and Fabulous: ‘Cats: The Jellicle Ball’ on Broadway
item.description: For their 10th life, the cats strut and duckwalk in a reappraisal of Andrew Lloyd Webber’s 1981 musical, which has shifted to the queer ballroom scene.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw and Sara Krulwich

item.title: Norman Bussel, Who Chronicled Veterans’ Unseen Traumas, Dies at 102
item.description: After a grueling year in a German prison camp during World War II, he endured crushing nightmares and survivor guilt back home, leading him to spread the word about veterans’ suffering.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Peter Schrag Dies at 94; Wrote of Dangers of California’s Populist Streak
item.description: His best-received book explored the state’s infatuation with voter initiatives, which were sometimes pushed with anti-immigrant fervor.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.category: Paradise Lost: California's Experience, America's Future (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Ending Writes Itself,’ by Evelyn Clarke
item.description: In this novel, a group of authors race to finish a mystery manuscript, only to find themselves part of a lethal plot.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Harlan

item.title: Book Review: ‘Hexes of the Deadwood Forest,’ by Agnieszka Szpila
item.description: The Polish best seller “Hexes of the Deadwood Forest” is like a post-porn fever dream of Eastern European magic realism crossed with a plant-based “Joy of Sex.”
item.dc:creator: Sadie Stein

item.title: Book Review: ‘American Fantasy,’ by Emma Straub
item.description: In Emma Straub’s latest novel, “American Fantasy,” a pop group’s midlife return provides fodder for both comedy and redemption on the high seas.
item.dc:creator: Leah Greenblatt

item.title: How Did Black Music Take Over the World? Let Melvin Gibbs Explain.
item.description: Since the late ’70s, the bassist has worked to map a musical route that mirrored the trans-Atlantic slave trade and birthed nearly all of American popular music.
item.dc:creator: Hank Shteamer
item.category: How Black Music Took Over the World (Book)
item.media:credit: Galen Fletcher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Melvin Gibbs has become one of his generation’s most admired and least easily categorized instrumentalists and conceptualists.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Corto Maltese,’ by Hugo Pratt
item.description: “Corto Maltese,” Hugo Pratt’s influential 1967 graphic novel, returns, with just as much to say about childhood during wartime.
item.dc:creator: Sam Thielman
item.category: Corto Maltese: Fable of Venice and Other Adventures (Book)

item.title: Ronald H. Spector, Who Traced Social History in Books on War, Dies at 83
item.description: A Vietnam veteran-turned-academic historian, he drew acclaim for portraying conflicts from the perspectives of generals as well as grunts on all sides, both in Vietnam and in World War II.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Oyster Diaries,’ by Nancy Lemann
item.description: The well-born protagonist of Nancy Lemann’s novel “The Oyster Diaries” returns home and immediately feels like an outsider.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner
item.media:description: Nancy Lemann writes about women who are frank, sardonic, bookish and neurotic. She channels their discontent into slashing little thunderstorms of meaning, our critic writes.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Here Where We Live Is Our Country,’ by Molly Crabapple
item.description: In “Here Where We Live Is Our Country,” Molly Crabapple tells the story of a Jewish labor movement that fought antisemitism and nationalism with equal fervor.
item.dc:creator: Max Strasser
item.category: Here Where We Live Is Our Country: The Story of the Jewish Bund (Book)
